attributes.stem:
An otherwise well 48-year-old woman presents with a three-week history of a painless swelling in her neck. Her mother had thyroid cancer when she was 52, and her sister had a cyst in her neck removed five years ago.

On examination, there is a 3cm firm and non-tender swelling in the left anterior triangle of the neck. The swelling moves upwards on swallowing. Ultrasound shows that the lesion has well-defined borders.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigation findings would be most supportive of a diagnosis of thyroid malignancy in this patient?
options: Elevated serum calcitonin and CEA tumour marker
Neck ultrasound shows an anechoic lesion with a regular, round shape and acoustic shadowing
TFTs show reduced TSH and elevated T4
FNA cytology shows nuclear grooves, intra-nuclear inclusions with dystrophic calcification
Scintigraphy scan reveals a 'cold' nodule with decreased tracer uptake
attributes.explanation:
There are numerous types of thyroid malignancy with papillary thyroid cancers being one of the most common, along with follicular cancers. Both are a type of well-differentiated thyroid carcinoma. On histology, papillary carcinomas will often demonstrate characteristic findings of nuclear grooves, intra-nuclear inclusions, dystrophic calcification, psammoma bodies and "Orphan Annie" nuclei.

An elevated serum calcitonin and CEA are markers characteristically elevated in medullary thyroid carcinoma, which is a cancer of the thyroid C cells. Malignant lesions typically appear hypoechoic on ultrasound and have irregular, poorly defined borders. A regular shaped lesion with acoustic shadowing is more typical of a thyroid cyst. A reduced TSH and elevated T4 would be in keeping with hyperthyroidism, particularly Grave's Disease. TFTs are normal in thyroid cancer, but are an important investigation to rule out a functional nodule.

A 'cold' nodule is described as an area of reduced tracer uptake. It is important to remember that only well-differentiated thyroid cancers can take up radioactive iodine, so scintigraphy is not useful for assessment of suspected poorly-differentiated thyroid cancers. Furthermore, it cannot differentiate between papillary and follicular thyroid cancers, as both appear 'cold' on the scan. The vast majority (95%) of cold thyroid nodules are benign.

A radioiodine scan cannot be used to categorically exclude carcinoma. Whilst most hot or warm nodules will be benign, some hot nodules do contain carcinoma. Whilst most cancers will be non-functional in terms of not taking up radioiodine, there are other explanations such as cysts which might explain the 'cold' status of a nodule. There radionucleide scanning is not the most reliable means of determining the presence of thyroid malignancy.
learningPoints: Psammoma bodies are characteristic of papillary thyroid cancers.
references: Chadwick D.R., Tani S.I. Management of the thyroid nodule. Surgery. 2017;35(10):563-568. Available at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319437942_Management_of_the_thyroid_nodule

Balasubramanian S.P., Thomas W.E. Thyroid neoplasms (including the solitary nodule) Surgery. 2007; 25(11):482-486. Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263931907002542
Bomeli SR, LeBeau SO, Ferris RL. Evaluation of a thyroid nodule. Otolaryngol Clin North Am. 2010; 43: 229–38. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20510711/
